Even in the most terrible events, even when they involve the worst crimes of people, we can see God’s truth and justice. He never has nor can command anything unjust or unreasonable. Jehu destroyed everyone who remained of Ahab's family; all those who shared in his wickedness. When we think about the suffering and misery of humanity, when we look forward to the resurrection and final judgment, and consider the great number of wicked people waiting for their terrible sentence of eternal fire; when everything about death and misery has been considered, a serious question arises: Who killed all these people? The answer is, sin. Should we then keep sin in our hearts and seek happiness from that which causes all misery?

Is your heart right? This is a question we should often ask ourselves. I make a good profession, have earned respect among people, but is my heart right? Am I sincere with God? Jehonadab supported Jehu in both his act of revenge and his efforts at reform. An upright heart pleases God and seeks nothing more than His acceptance; but if we aim for the praise of people, we are building on a false foundation. Whether Jehu looked for anything further, we cannot judge. The law of God clearly stated that idolaters were to be put to death. In this way, idolatry was removed from Israel for the time being. May we desire that it be uprooted from our hearts.

It is rightly questioned whether Jehu acted from a good motive, and whether he did not take some wrong steps in doing so; yet no service done for God will go unrewarded. But true conversion involves more than just turning away from obvious sins; it involves turning away from all sin. It’s not only about abandoning false gods, but also false worship. True conversion is not only about giving up wasteful sins, but also profitable ones; not only about avoiding sins that harm our worldly interests, but those that support and benefit them; in forsaking which is the great test of whether we can deny ourselves and trust God. Jehu showed great care and zeal for eliminating a false religion, but he did not care about true religion, took no thought to please God and do his duty. Those who are careless are likely without grace. The people were also careless, so it’s not surprising that the Lord began to reduce Israel's size during those days. They failed in their duties to God, therefore God reduced their extent, wealth, and power.
